sourceMovie.title: Psycho
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Psycho works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Dressed to Kill
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Psycho clicked for you, Dressed to Kill (1980) is a strong next stop: both trade in horror and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Memento
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Psycho clicked for you, Memento (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and mystery, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Psycho?
faq.answer: Try Dressed to Kill, Goodnight Mommy, Memento—each shares DNA with Psycho while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Psycho?
faq.answer: Psycho (1960) is best described as Horror, Thriller, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Psycho part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Psycho Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
